title: Wire fromSpec(.auto/.git_helper/.host_capability) through SpecOptions
description:

To wire them, SpecOptions needs to carry: gpa (already there), io (Io), parent_env (*const Environ.Map) for subprocess sources, and an optional HostDispatcher for host_capability. The auto walker also needs the per-platform default chain documented in docs/design/adrs/2026-04-29-github-api-refstore.md § 3 (native: explicit > env > gh > keychain > git; WASM: explicit > host_capability).

Acceptance:
- fromSpec(.gh_helper) constructs and returns a working GhHelperSource.
- fromSpec(.git_helper) constructs and returns a working GitHelperSource.
- fromSpec(.host_capability) constructs and returns a working HostCapabilitySource (WASM-only — non-WASM should return error.HelperUnavailable from the source itself, not from fromSpec).
- fromSpec(.auto) walks the per-platform default chain and returns the first available credential, mapping HelperUnavailable to fall-through and any other CredentialError to short-circuit.
- credential_provider_test.zig grows fromSpec round-trip tests for each new arm.
notes: Shipped in https://github.com/sideshowdb/sideshowdb/pull/33 (commits e23e923 + ddd9a68 + 9adc0d6).

title: WASM standalone RefStore: wire MemoryRefStore as default in WASM build
description:
Make WASM document ops work with no host bridge by defaulting to MemoryRefStore in src/wasm/root.zig and making ImportedRefStore opt-in.

Why:
- After MemoryRefStore lands, WASM should not require a JS hostBridge to demo document put/get/list/history/delete.
- Keep ImportedRefStore available for hosts that want to persist beyond the WASM instance (IndexedDB story).

Scope:
- Replace the unconditional ImportedRefStore wiring with a runtime/init-time selector. Default backend = MemoryRefStore. Selector exposed to host (e.g. opts in loadSideshowdbClient or a sideshowdb_set_backend export) so hosts can switch to ImportedRefStore explicitly.
- TypeScript bindings: hostBridge becomes optional and the docs update accordingly. When omitted, client uses in-WASM memory store. host-bridge error path only fires when the user opted into ImportedRefStore.
- Update bindings/typescript/sideshowdb-core tests: cover no-bridge happy path including put/get/list/history/delete round trip.
- Update README playground/quick-start to lead with the no-bridge example.

EARS:
- When loadSideshowdbClient is called without hostBridge, the client shall default to the WASM in-process MemoryRefStore.
- Where hostBridge is provided, the client shall route ref operations through the host bridge instead of MemoryRefStore.
- If hostBridge is provided but a ref op throws, then the client shall return a host-bridge error result without mutating MemoryRefStore state.

Acceptance:
- 'zig build wasm' produces an artifact whose document_put/get/list/history/delete work end-to-end with no JS imports beyond memory ones.
- TypeScript acceptance suite covers no-bridge round-trip.
- README quick-start example does not pass hostBridge.
close_reason: WASM defaults to MemoryRefStore via runtime selector (commit 15db230). hostBridge optional; loadSideshowdbClient flips backend when bridge is supplied

title: WASM standalone RefStore: in-memory backend (Option A)
description:
Add freestanding-safe MemoryRefStore in src/core so WASM can run document ops without a host bridge.

Why:
- src/wasm/root.zig only wires ImportedRefStore today, so any WASM consumer must implement a JS host bridge.
- src/core/storage.zig gates ZiggitRefStore to non-freestanding (filesystem-bound), so ziggit work did not give WASM standalone capability.
- MVP playground/demo story needs WASM to just work after loadSideshowdbClient with no host wiring.

Scope:
- Implement MemoryRefStore with full RefStore vtable (put/get/delete/list/history) using only std + allocator (no FS, no process, no extern). Append-only version history per key.
- Compiles for wasm32-freestanding. Native build also exposes it (useful for tests).
- Unit tests covering happy/negative/edge/boundary per CLAUDE.md TDD bar (empty key, empty value, missing key get, version-pinned get, history ordering, delete-then-list, overwrite preserves prior version).
- EARS in description.

EARS:
- The MemoryRefStore shall return a unique VersionId for each successful put.
- When get is called with a known key and no version, the MemoryRefStore shall return the most recently put value.
- When get is called with a key that has no entries, the MemoryRefStore shall return null.
- When get is called with a version that does not exist for the key, the MemoryRefStore shall return null.
- When delete is called for a known key, the MemoryRefStore shall remove the key from list output and from future no-version get calls.
- The MemoryRefStore shall preserve historical versions of a key after subsequent puts so history returns them in put order.
- If the store is dropped, then all data shall be lost (volatile by design, documented).

Acceptance:
- 'zig build test' green on native including MemoryRefStore unit tests.
- 'zig build wasm' compiles MemoryRefStore into wasm32-freestanding artifact.
- No new dependencies.
close_reason: MemoryRefStore landed in mvp-wasm-standalone branch (commit 19d731c)

title: GitHubApiRefStore: document deinitCaches + enable_read_caching contract
description:
## Why

`deinitCaches` frees ref-tip ETag state and object-body maps but is **not** invoked automatically when a `GitHubApiRefStore` goes out of scope (Zig has no RAII on structs). Tests opt out of read caching via `enable_read_caching=false` to avoid allocator leaks; production embedders need a **clear contract** for when to enable caching, when to call `deinitCaches`, and whether the native CLI should tie this to process lifetime.

## What

- Document `enable_read_caching` default (`true` in `Options`) vs test harness (`false` in `initGitHubStore` helper) in design or operations docs (link from `site/.../configuration` or core storage docs as appropriate).
- Document `deinitCaches` for long-lived CLI / embedding scenarios.
- Optionally: add a short comment block in `github_api_ref_store.zig` pointing to the doc.

## Acceptance

- A reader can answer: "If I turn on read caching in a test or CLI, how do I avoid leaks?" without reading Discord history.

title: RefStore / GitHubApiRefStore: rate-limit metadata for list, delete, history
description:
## Why

GHAPI-071 calls for surfacing rate-limit headers on **remote results**. We extended `RefStore.ReadResult` for `get` only. `list` returns `[][]u8`, `delete` returns `void`, and `history` returns `[]VersionId` — none carry `RateLimitInfo`, so callers cannot observe upstream throttling on those paths without ad hoc logging.

## What

Choose and implement **one** approach (document in ADR or issue before coding):

1. **Widen `RefStore` vtable** with result structs that include optional `rate_limit` (breaking for all implementors), or
2. **GitHub-only extended methods** (e.g. `listWithMeta`) alongside the vtable, or
3. **Explicitly defer** and document "get-only" until WASM/TS needs force the decision.

## Acceptance

- Decision recorded (comment on `sideshowdb-p4p` or small ADR note).
- If implemented: TypeScript/WASM consumers can read rate-limit state for the chosen operations; `MemoryRefStore` / `SubprocessGitRefStore` remain `null`/no-op.

title: GitHubApiRefStore: wire ObjectBodyCache into list, history, delete reads
description:
## Why

Phase 9.2 requires **all read paths** to consult SHA-keyed caches, not only `get`. Today `list`, `history`, and the read portion of `delete` still issue fresh GETs for commit/tree (and related) even when bodies are already in `ObjectBodyCache` from a prior `get`.

## What

- Refactor shared commit/tree fetch helpers where practical so `list` / `history` / `delete` hit the same cache insert/lookup paths as `readBlobForKeyAtCommit`.
- Add request-count assertions in `tests/github_api_refstore_test.zig` (warm path) for at least `list` and one `history` pagination case.

## Acceptance

- No behavioral regressions vs current GitHub API semantics.
- `zig build test` green; tests that enable read caching either call `deinitCaches` or keep caching disabled except targeted cases (avoid leaks).

title: GitHubApiRefStore: integration test — tree/blob reuse after ref tip changes
description:
## Why

`get_warm_cache_serves_304` proves **304 on the ref** plus reuse of cached **immutable** objects. A separate scenario from the plan (Phase 9.1) is: the **ref tip advances** to a new commit SHA, but the **underlying tree/blob SHAs** for a key are unchanged; we must still avoid redundant GETs for those objects.

## What

- Add `cache_test_tree_hit_avoids_request` (or equivalent name) per plan: second `get` after tip change still reuses cached blob/tree bodies when SHAs match.
- Likely requires QueuedTransport sequences that return a new ref tip commit with a tree that still references the same blob SHA.

## Acceptance

- Second `get` issues **no** GET `/git/blobs/{sha}` (and ideally no redundant tree GET) when blob/tree SHAs are unchanged.
- Documented in test comments referencing GHAPI / plan Phase 9.1.

title: GitHubApiRefStore: LRU/byte-budget ShaCache + eviction tests
description:
## Why

`ObjectBodyCache` currently stores duped JSON bodies in **unbounded** `StringHashMapUnmanaged` maps. A long-lived process or hostile workload could grow memory without bound.

## What

- Implement the plan’s generic **LRU (or byte-budget) ShaCache** for commit/tree/blob bodies (see Phase 9.1 in `docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-29-github-api-refstore-implementation.md`).
- Replace or wrap `ObjectBodyCache` with that implementation.
- Add `cache_test_blob_lru_eviction` in `tests/github_api_cache_test.zig` (or adjacent) per plan.

## Acceptance

- Eviction removes the oldest / least-recently-used entry when over budget.
- `zig build test` green; no new leaks under `std.testing.allocator` when caching is enabled in tests.

title: Investigate usage-cli config metadata support for CLI specs
description: During sideshowdb-2t0 we found that usage-cli 3.2.1 rejects top-level config_file and flag-level config= metadata even though the published spec docs describe those forms. The canonical src/cli/usage/sideshowdb.usage.kdl therefore documents refstore config precedence in long_help text instead of first-class usage metadata. Investigate whether a different supported syntax exists, whether we should pin a different usage release, or whether we need a local post-processing convention for config-backed docs/manpages.
notes:
## Investigation findings (2026-04-30)

### Reproduction with usage 3.2.1

Top-level config_file:
  × Invalid usage config: unsupported spec key config_file

Flag-level config=:
  × Invalid usage config: unsupported flag key config

### Upstream state (jdx/usage)

- 3.2.1 (released 2026-04-22) is current latest. No newer release exists.
- Source review of lib/src/spec/mod.rs and lib/src/spec/flag.rs on main:
  - Top-level keys accepted: name, bin, version, author, source_code_link_template, about, long_about, about_long, about_md, license, before_help, after_help, before_long_help, before_help_long, after_long_help, after_help_long, usage, arg, flag, cmd, config, complete, disable_help, min_usage_version, default_subcommand, example, include
  - Flag-level keys accepted (inline + child): help, long_help, help_long, help_md, required, var, var_min, var_max, hide, deprecated, global, count, default, env, negate, choices, arg
- config_file (top-level) and config= (flag-level) have NEVER been implemented in any released usage version. Published doc references appear aspirational.
- No open issues, PRs, or proposals upstream tracking flag→config linkage.

### What IS supported: top-level config { prop } block (added v3.0.0)

Verified parses cleanly with usage 3.2.1:

  config {
      prop "refstore.kind" default="subprocess" env="SIDESHOWDB_REFSTORE" help="Backend"
  }

Properties on each prop: default, default_note, data_type, env, help, long_help.
This is a config-key registry, not per-flag binding. Markdown generator does not currently render the block visibly but the data is part of the parsed spec.

### Recommendation

1. Adopt top-level config { prop ... } block in src/cli/usage/sideshowdb.usage.kdl as the machine-readable home for config-file precedence metadata (refstore.kind, etc).
2. Keep per-flag long_help prose for human-facing config docs (current convention). No machine-readable flag→config linkage exists.
3. Update src/cli/usage/root.zig to recognize the config block. Two options:
   - Add "config" to isIgnoredTopLevelNode for silent passthrough (minimal change; usage CLI still sees it for any future renderer support).
   - Parse it into a Spec.config_props field exposed to handlers (lets us drive runtime precedence resolution from the spec instead of hard-coded code).
4. Do NOT pin a newer usage version — 3.2.1 is latest, and no upstream proposal for flag→config linkage. If we want machine-readable per-flag config binding we would need to (a) propose upstream, or (b) use a private custom-extension node and strip it before invoking usage generate.

### Follow-up work to file

- Spec migration: add config { prop ... } block, expand long_help for flags
- Parser: extend root.zig to ingest config block (decide: ignore vs. parse into Spec)
- (Optional) upstream proposal for flag-level config= binding in jdx/usage
close_reason: Investigation complete. Findings recorded in issue notes. usage 3.2.1 is the latest release; config_file (top-level) and config= (flag-level) are unsupported and have never been implemented. Supported alternative: top-level config { prop ... } block (added v3.0.0). Long-term plan: migrate spec to use the block (sideshowdb-nkk) and teach the Zig parser to accept it (sideshowdb-3ob). Per-flag long_help prose remains the convention for human-facing config docs since no machine-readable flag→config linkage exists upstream.

title: SHA-keyed in-memory caches for commit/tree/blob
description:
## Scope (Phase 9 — SHA-keyed caches)

This issue tracks **immutable Git object body caching** (commit / tree / blob JSON) and wiring it through **all read-heavy RefStore paths**, not only `get`.

### Already landed (partial implementation)

- `src/core/storage/github_api/cache.zig` defines `ObjectBodyCache`: SHA-keyed maps holding **allocator-owned** JSON response bodies for `/git/commits/{sha}`, `/git/trees/{sha}`, and `/git/blobs/{sha}`.
- `readBlobForKeyAtCommit` (used by `GitHubApiRefStore.get`) consults and populates those maps when `Options.enable_read_caching` is **true**.
- Unit tests default `enable_read_caching` to **false** in `initGitHubStore(..., false)` so the Zig test allocator does not leak cached bodies; `get_warm_cache_serves_304` opts in with `true` and calls `deinitCaches` in a `defer`.
- Current maps are **unbounded** (no LRU / byte budget yet).

### Reasoning

GitHub Git Database objects are **content-addressed and immutable**: caching by SHA is safe across ref tip changes and avoids redundant bytes on the wire when the same commit/tree/blob appears in multiple operations (e.g. repeated `get`, `list`, paginated `history`).

### Remaining work (see child tasks)

1. **Bounded eviction** — Replace or wrap `ObjectBodyCache` with the plan’s generic **LRU / byte-budget** `ShaCache`, plus `cache_test_blob_lru_eviction`.
2. **Tip-change reuse** — Add `cache_test_tree_hit_avoids_request`: ref advances to a new commit whose tree still references the **same** blob SHA; second `get` must not re-GET that blob (and ideally reuses tree JSON when SHA matches).
3. **Wire caches beyond `get`** — `list`, `history`, and the **read** portion of `delete` should hit the same object cache and gain **request-count** tests analogous to the warm-cache integration test.

### References

- Plan: `docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-29-github-api-refstore-implementation.md` Phase 9.
- EARS: GHAPI-034 and read-path performance expectations in the GitHub API RefStore spec.

title: ETag ref tip cache + rate-limit header surface
description:
## Scope (Phase 8 — ETag ref tip + rate-limit surface + related lifecycle)

This issue tracks **conditional ref reads** (ETag / 304), **surfacing rate-limit metadata** from GitHub responses, and **caller-facing lifecycle** for cache state tied to those features.

### Already landed (partial implementation)

- **Ref tip ETag cache** (`RefTipCache` in `github_api/cache.zig`): stores `{ commit_sha, etag }` for the configured ref; `readTipCommitSha` sends `If-None-Match` when known; **304** reuses cached tip SHA; **200** refreshes cache when `Response.etag` is present; ref cache is **invalidated** after successful `put` / `delete` ref updates.
- **Warm read integration**: `get_warm_cache_serves_304` proves a second `get` can issue **one** conditional ref GET while reusing cached commit/tree/blob bodies.
- **`ReadResult.rate_limit`**: `RefStore.ReadResult` now carries optional `rate_limit`; `GitHubApiRefStore.get` merges the **last** upstream `X-RateLimit-*` parse along the commit→tree→blob walk. `MemoryRefStore` / `SubprocessGitRefStore` leave it unset.
- **`PutResult.rate_limit`** was already present from PUT work (GHAPI-071).

### Gaps / follow-ups (reasoning)

1. **Rate-limit on other RefStore operations** — Today only **`get`** returns `ReadResult` with optional rate metadata. `list`, `delete`, and `history` still use the legacy vtable shapes (`void`, `[][]u8`, `[]VersionId`) with **no** rate-limit channel. Either extend the `RefStore` contract (breaking-ish) or introduce parallel GitHub-specific APIs; needs an explicit product/design decision so GHAPI-071 is not silently “get-only” forever.

2. **Cache lifecycle for embedders** — `GitHubApiRefStore.deinitCaches` frees ref-tip state and object maps. Long-lived callers (future native CLI process, tests with `enable_read_caching=true`) need a **documented contract**: when to call `deinitCaches`, whether the CLI should call it on shutdown, and whether WASM bindings ever enable in-memory caching without a host teardown hook.

3. **`enable_read_caching` flag** — Defaults **on** in `Options` for production-shaped builds; tests pass **false** to avoid allocator leaks under `std.testing.allocator`. Document when embedders should disable vs enable, and whether CLI exposes a flag.

### References

- Plan: `docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-29-github-api-refstore-implementation.md` Phase 8 (Tasks 8.1–8.2).
- ADR / EARS: GHAPI-034 (304 reuse), GHAPI-071 (rate-limit surface).
close_reason: Core Phase 8–9 caching shipped in https://github.com/sideshowdb/sideshowdb/pull/42 (ETag ref tip, LRU object bodies, get rate_limit). Parent close forced: open children sideshowdb-p4p.1 and sideshowdb-p4p.2 track deliberate follow-ups (vtable rate-limit shape; embedder cache lifecycle docs).

title: Decision trace: hostCapabilities.store (TypeScript WASM)
description: Canonical rationale for PR #24: nest host ref persistence under hostCapabilities.store; rename bridge/connector language to host store; document trace in docs/design/adrs/2026-04-29-host-capabilities-store-api.md, README, AGENTS, site /docs/design/, and core README. Search: hostCapabilities, host store ADR.
design:
# ADR — TypeScript WASM `hostCapabilities.store` and host store naming

- **Date:** 2026-04-29
- **Status:** Accepted
- **PR:** [sideshowdb#24](https://github.com/sideshowdb/sideshowdb/pull/24)
- **Supersedes:** Flat `hostBridge` / `hostConnector` options and file names
  using `*bridge*` for the ref persistence adapter in `@sideshowdb/core`.

## 1. Context

The browser WASM client needs the host (JavaScript) to supply a **ref
store** implementation when the module is configured for imported ref
storage. Early bindings exposed that dependency as a **top-level option**
with names like `hostBridge` and types like `IndexedDbRefHostBridge`.

That naming carried two problems:

1. **Metaphor mismatch.** The object is not a “bridge” in the network sense; it
   is a **store** implementing the same conceptual contract as other ref
   stores (put/get/list/history/delete). Calling it a bridge obscures the
   mental model and drifts from Zig-side vocabulary (`RefStore`).
2. **Extension shape.** We expect additional **host-provided capabilities**
   over time (for example sync helpers, telemetry hooks, or platform file
   access). A flat `hostBridge` option does not compose: every new concern
   would force another top-level rename or ambiguous overload.

We therefore needed a **stable container** for host-side capabilities and a
**honest name** for the ref persistence implementation.

## 2. Options considered

### 2.1 Keep `hostBridge` and add parallel top-level options

**Pros.** No breaking change for early adopters.

**Cons.** Does not scale; option bag becomes a flat grab bag; names stay
misleading.

### 2.2 Rename to `hostConnector` only

**Pros.** Slightly more generic.

**Cons.** Still a flat option; “connector” is equally vague; does not reserve
space for future capabilities.

### 2.3 Introduce `hostCapabilities: { store?: SideshowdbHostStore }`

**Pros.**

- **Accurate language:** `SideshowdbHostStore` matches behavior — a ref store
  supplied by the host.
- **Forward compatible:** New capabilities nest under `hostCapabilities`
  without breaking existing callers that only pass `store`.
- **Aligns with errors:** A dedicated `host-store` error kind mirrors the
  capability name.

**Cons.** Breaking rename for TypeScript callers and file/module names
(`indexeddb-bridge` → `indexeddb-store`, demo and acceptance helpers).

## 3. Decision

Adopt **2.3**:

- `LoadSideshowdbClientOptions.hostCapabilities?: { store?: SideshowdbHostStore }`
- Public types and factories use **store** language (`IndexedDbHostStore`,
  `createIndexedDbHostStore`, `createIndexedDbHostStoreEffect`).
- IndexedDB persistence ships as `indexeddb-store` (module/file naming) while
  acceptance feature filenames may retain legacy `*-bridge*` segments until
  renamed deliberately with scenario mapping updates.

Default wiring when IndexedDB exists and the caller did not supply
`hostCapabilities.store` remains **automatic**; the change is naming and
option shape, not persistence semantics.

## 4. Consequences

- **Callers** pass `hostCapabilities: { store: myStore }` instead of a flat
  bridge/connector property.
- **Documentation** points here and to PR #24 for the full diff and
  validation notes.
- **Beads:** Chore `sideshowdb-ywt` (`external_ref: gh-pr-24`) embeds this ADR
  in `design` notes so agents searching “host capabilities” find the trace.
- **EARS** documents under `docs/development/specs/` should use the same
  vocabulary (`hostCapabilities.store`, host store) when describing
  user-visible behavior.

## 5. References

- TypeScript EARS: `docs/development/specs/typescript-bindings-and-wasm-browser-bridge-ears.md`
- IndexedDB EARS: `docs/development/specs/indexeddb-host-bridge-ears.md`
- Core package README: `bindings/typescript/sideshowdb-core/README.md`
close_reason: Trace captured in docs/design (ADR), PR #24, README/AGENTS/CLAUDE/core README, site /docs/design/, EARS; bd remember ts-host-capabilities-store.

title: Browser persistence + sync: IndexedDB-backed RefStore
description:
Persist browser WASM RefStore state across reloads with an IndexedDB-backed default host bridge in @sideshowdb/core, while preserving the existing host-bridge override seam for advanced integrations.

Why:
- MemoryRefStore in WASM is volatile; browser users otherwise lose state on reload.
- The architecture spec treats browser IndexedDB as a disposable local materialization layer and natural persistence target.
- We need deterministic backend selection before remote sync lands so browser behavior is predictable.

Scope:
- Implement a default IndexedDB-backed RefStore host bridge in @sideshowdb/core.
- Backend selection precedence for browser runtime:
  1) Explicit hostBridge option (caller-provided) wins.
  2) Otherwise, IndexedDB default bridge when indexedDB is available.
  3) Otherwise, fallback to in-WASM MemoryRefStore.
- Define IndexedDB schema contract for Option A:
  - Object store keyed by RefStore key.
  - Value shape stores append-only version history entries [{ version, value }] per key.
- On client boot, hydrate in-memory bridge cache from IndexedDB before first document operation.
- On put/delete, serialize persistence writes to preserve operation order and avoid out-of-order durable state.
- Expose bridge constructor for advanced callers while keeping browser default automatic.

Out of scope:
- Remote push/pull protocol and conflict resolution semantics (tracked in sideshowdb-ig3).
- Ziggit virtual-FS parity layout work (tracked in sideshowdb-dgz).

EARS:
- When loadSideshowdbClient is called in a browser-like runtime without a hostBridge and indexedDB is available, the Sideshowdb TypeScript client shall route RefStore operations through a default IndexedDB-backed host bridge.
- When loadSideshowdbClient is called with an explicit hostBridge, the Sideshowdb TypeScript client shall use the explicit hostBridge instead of the default IndexedDB bridge.
- If indexedDB is unavailable and no explicit hostBridge is provided, then the Sideshowdb TypeScript client shall continue using the in-WASM MemoryRefStore.
- When the IndexedDB-backed bridge starts, it shall hydrate key/version history state from IndexedDB before serving RefStore reads.
- When consecutive puts target the same key, the IndexedDB-backed bridge shall persist the resulting versions in operation order.
- When a page reload occurs after at least one successful put through the default IndexedDB bridge, subsequent gets shall return the persisted value for that key.
acceptance_criteria:
- Browser default persistence: in browser-like tests with IndexedDB available and no hostBridge provided, a put in one client instance is readable from a new client instance after reload simulation.
- Precedence: when hostBridge is provided, client routes through hostBridge and does not rely on default IndexedDB behavior.
- Fallback: when IndexedDB is unavailable and no hostBridge is provided, document ops continue to succeed via in-WASM MemoryRefStore semantics.
- Ordering: sequential puts persist in deterministic order, and history/get reflect the latest write after reload.
- Public API/docs: @sideshowdb/core docs describe IndexedDB as browser default and document opt-out/fallback behavior.
notes: REST pivot: see ADR docs/design/adrs/2026-04-29-github-api-refstore.md. IndexedDB host store remains the canonical browser-local persistence; cache reuse is filed as a follow-up under the new epic.

title: Remote sync: push/pull RefStore to a remote git endpoint
description:
Sync the local RefStore to a remote git repository so multiple sideshowdb clients (CLI and WASM) can converge on shared state.

Why:
- MVP value prop is event-sourced + offline-friendly + Git-backed; without a sync path the Git backing is invisible to users.
- Unblocks collaborative scenarios for both native (server) and browser (peer) clients.

Scope:
- Define sync semantics: which ref(s) are exchanged, conflict policy (fast-forward only vs merge of append-only history), authentication surface (https token, ssh).
- Add 'sideshowdb sync push <remote>' and 'sideshowdb sync pull <remote>' CLI commands.
- Native impl uses ziggit (preferred) or git subprocess fallback.
- Document sync behavior in README and docs/development/specs.
- EARS-driven design doc + acceptance tests (CLI cucumber + integration).

Out of scope here: IndexedDB-backed sync (separate issue), conflict-free CRDT layer.

EARS (initial draft):
- When 'sync push' is called against a configured remote, the CLI shall upload the local RefStore ref so a fresh clone receives the same document state.
- When 'sync pull' is called against a configured remote, the CLI shall fetch the remote ref and fast-forward the local RefStore.
- If the remote ref has diverged from the local ref, then the CLI shall fail with a clear non-zero error and leave local state unchanged.
- Where authentication is required, the CLI shall accept credentials via standard git mechanisms (SSH agent, credential helper, env tokens).

Acceptance:
- Roundtrip: client A puts doc, sync push; client B sync pull, get returns same doc + version history.
- Divergence test fails predictably.
- README and a dedicated 'docs/development/specs/sync-spec.md' describe the contract.

title: Fix clean-checkout JS test prerequisites for WASM bindings
description:
The root Bun workspace verification path is not clean-checkout safe. In a fresh worktree, both `bun run test` and `zig build js:test` fail before exercising the TypeScript bindings because the workspace assumes prebuilt artifacts already exist. The core binding tests read `zig-out/wasm/sideshowdb.wasm`, and the Effect package resolves `@sideshowdb/core` through its built `dist` export, so a fresh checkout needs both the WASM artifact and built binding outputs before root JS tests can pass.

EARS:
- When `zig build js:test` runs in a clean checkout, the repo shall build any WASM and binding-package artifacts required by the JS workspace tests before running them.
- When the root Bun workspace test command runs in a clean checkout, the TypeScript binding test suites shall not fail with missing `zig-out/wasm/sideshowdb.wasm` or unresolved workspace package exports.
- If JS workspace tests depend on generated artifacts, then the repo shall either generate those artifacts automatically or load test fixtures that do not require manual prebuild steps.

Acceptance:
- In a fresh worktree with no preexisting `zig-out/wasm/sideshowdb.wasm` and no binding `dist/` outputs, `zig build js:test` passes without any manual prerequisite commands.
- The root verification path documents or encodes the correct prerequisite order instead of relying on stale local build outputs.
- The fix preserves build.zig as the top-level orchestrator for the JS test workflow.
close_reason: Encoded clean-checkout-safe JS workspace prerequisites in root check/test scripts and wired zig build js:test to explicit wasm + binding build dependencies; verified with bash scripts/verify-js-workspace.sh and zig build js:check after deleting binding dist outputs and the wasm artifact.

title: CLI stdout writer overwrites prior file content when redirected
description: When the sideshowdb CLI is invoked multiple times with stdout redirected to the same regular file via shell { ... } > file aggregation, each invocation appears to seek to offset 0 and overwrite prior content rather than appending to the inherited file position. Repro: { echo '{...}' | sideshowdb --json doc put ... ; sideshowdb --json doc get ... ; sideshowdb --json doc delete ... ; sideshowdb --json doc list ... ; } > /tmp/log 2>&1 — the resulting file ends up only ~155 bytes instead of the expected ~490+ bytes. Workaround: pipe each invocation through cat. Likely cause: src/cli/main.zig std.Io.File.Writer.init(.stdout(), ...) initializing a fresh writer that resets position 0 when stdout is a regular file.
acceptance_criteria: CLI binary preserves shell-inherited stdout position; chained invocations into one redirect concatenate cleanly without trailing overwrite; regression test exercising { put; get; delete; list; } > file flow.
notes: Fix submitted in PR #16 (https://github.com/sideshowdb/sideshowdb/pull/16). Awaiting review/merge before closing.

title: Make ziggit the default selectable RefStore backend
description:
Promote the ziggit-backed RefStore work from the closed exploration branch into the production storage path. The native ziggit backend should become the default non-freestanding GitRefStore while the existing subprocess implementation remains available as an explicitly selectable fallback for compatibility and debugging. CLI users must be able to select the backend via command-line flag, environment variable, and repo-local config, with clear precedence and clear failures for invalid backend names.

EARS:
- The Sideshowdb native storage layer shall default to a ziggit-backed GitRefStore on non-freestanding targets.
- The Sideshowdb native storage layer shall keep the subprocess-backed Git implementation available as an explicit fallback backend.
- When a CLI user passes --refstore ziggit or --refstore subprocess, the Sideshowdb CLI shall use the requested backend for document operations.
- When no --refstore flag is passed and SIDESHOWDB_REFSTORE is set, the Sideshowdb CLI shall use the backend named by that environment variable.
- When no --refstore flag or environment override is provided and .sideshowdb/config.toml contains [storage] refstore, the Sideshowdb CLI shall use the configured backend.
- The Sideshowdb CLI shall resolve backend selection in this precedence order: command-line flag, environment variable, config file, built-in default.
- If a backend selector names an unsupported backend, then the Sideshowdb CLI shall fail with a clear usage/configuration error and shall not mutate document refs.
- The ziggit-backed backend shall preserve the existing RefStore put, get, delete, list, and history behavior, including commit-SHA VersionId values.
- The subprocess-backed fallback shall remain covered by the same RefStore parity suite as the ziggit-backed backend.

Acceptance:
- GitRefStore resolves to the ziggit-backed backend by default on native targets.
- SubprocessGitRefStore remains available and tested.
- CLI backend selection supports --refstore, SIDESHOWDB_REFSTORE, and .sideshowdb/config.toml with documented precedence.
- Shared parity tests pass for ziggit and subprocess backends.
- Existing document, transport, CLI, WASM, and TypeScript verification remains green.
acceptance_criteria: Default native backend is ziggit; subprocess fallback is selectable; CLI flag/env/config precedence is tested; invalid backend names fail without mutation; parity tests cover both backends; full repo quality gates pass.
close_reason: All 7 implementation tasks complete; ZiggitRefStore default; SubprocessGitRefStore selectable via flag/env/config; parity tests pass; full regression green.

title: Tighten AutoBundle errdefer chain to deinit partial entries on construction failure
description: Code-review observation from sideshowdb-20m (commit 9adc0d6). credential_provider.zig:313-340 (and the WASM variant at :291) build the AutoBundle entries with successive try ... catches. If a later try fails, the errdefer frees the entries slice but does not run per-entry deinit on the entries that already succeeded. Today this is latent — every source's deinit is self.* = undefined no-op — but the next allocator-owning source (e.g. native keychain) will leak. Fix: write a deinitEntries(slice) helper and errdefer it after each successful entry assignment, mirroring the loop in ProviderHandle.deinit.

Offline writes: durable cache buffer for write-when-canonical-unreachable
description:
Allow writes to succeed while canonical (Git/remote) is unreachable, durably buffer them in a local cache, and flush to canonical when connectivity returns.

Why:
- SideshowDB is local-first by design. The browser playground, edge deployments, and any disconnected-by-default tooling need to accept writes while canonical is unreachable.
- Distinct from WriteThroughRefStore (sideshowdb-7pp) and WriteAroundRefStore (sideshowdb-p9h) — those both REQUIRE canonical to ack writes synchronously. This issue is the only path where caller-visible write success precedes canonical persistence.
- Overlaps in mechanism with WAL-batched flush (sideshowdb-0r1) — both need durable local buffering and ordered replay against canonical. Decide whether to share infrastructure or split when this issue is prioritised.

Scope:
- A composite mode (or distinct type) that, when canonical is reachable, behaves as WriteThroughRefStore; when canonical is unreachable, accepts writes into a durable local cache and acks the caller with a 'staged' VersionId.
- Reachability detection (configurable: explicit toggle, network probe, or canonical-error classification).
- Replay protocol: on canonical reconnect, replay buffered writes in order, return canonical version-ids back through any pending caller futures (or via reconciliation API).
- Conflict resolution policy when canonical has diverged during the offline window. Default: surface conflict; require operator/caller action.

Out of scope:
- Multi-writer offline merge (CRDT layer is separate).
- Automatic background sync intervals (operator concern).

Risks / open questions:
- Trust boundary: the staged VersionId is provisional. API must distinguish 'staged' from 'canonical' so callers don't confuse them.
- Crash semantics during the offline window: durable buffer must survive process restart and be replayed in order.

Depends on: sideshowdb-7pp

title: Write-around RefStore variant: bypass cache on writes
description:
Add a write-around composite RefStore: writes go straight to canonical, caches are populated only on read. Sibling to WriteThroughRefStore (sideshowdb-7pp).

Why:
- Realistic deployment is single-cache + read-perf + offline scenarios. Write-heavy paths don't benefit from cache pre-staging; eliminating the cache stage on writes simplifies the put path and eliminates speculative-cache windows.
- Trivial failure semantics: writes are exactly what canonical accepts. No `.lax`/`.strict` distinction for writes (only canonical can fail). No speculative cache state, no compensation, no list/get divergence under failure.
- Read path is identical to WriteThroughRefStore (read-through with refill).

Scope:
- New composite type (e.g. WriteAroundRefStore) sharing the read-through path with WriteThroughRefStore.
- Spec note distinguishing write-around vs write-through trade-offs and when to pick which.
- EARS: every write goes to canonical only; cache populated only via refill on read fall-through.
- Acceptance: parity harness against single-cache and three-cache topologies; cache loss recovery; cache pre-write absence after a successful put.

Out of scope:
- Offline write buffering (separate concern — see notes).
- Eviction policy.

Notes / future feature interaction:
- A future 'offline writes' mode (cached writes flushed when canonical is reachable) is additive on top of write-around or write-through. That mode requires a durable WAL, overlapping with sideshowdb-0r1 (WAL-batched flush). Track separately when prioritised.

Depends on: sideshowdb-7pp (composite primitive)

title: WAL-batched flush layer over WriteThroughRefStore
description:
Implement a write-ahead-log layer that wraps a WriteThroughRefStore (or similar canonical-backed RefStore) and batches multiple logical puts into one canonical commit. This is the *real* write-behind successor to sideshowdb-7pp — the throughput-optimization layer that the original 7pp wording described but the MVP composite intentionally did not deliver.

Scope:
- Durable WAL on disk (fsync semantics defined per OS).
- Crash-replay protocol: WAL entries not yet flushed to canonical are replayed at startup.
- Batched flusher: coalesces N WAL entries into one canonical commit.
- Idempotent replay: same WAL entry replayed twice produces the same canonical state.

Why split from sideshowdb-7pp:
- The composite primitive (sideshowdb-7pp) delivers a cache slot any backend can plug into. Zero durability regression.
- WAL+batching is an independent throughput optimization with its own EARS, format, and crash-recovery test plan. Bundling it into 7pp inflated scope and conflated two separable concerns. See docs/development/decisions/2026-04-29-caching-model.md for the full deliberation.

EARS (carried verbatim from original sideshowdb-7pp):
- When a write is accepted into the write-behind queue, the system shall flush the write to canonical Git/ref storage in deterministic order.
- If a write-behind flush fails, then the system shall surface a recoverable error state and shall not claim canonical commit success until Git/ref persistence succeeds.

Acceptance:
- Integration test: ordered writes flushed from the WAL queue are reflected in canonical Git/ref history in the same order.
- Recovery test: kill the process between WAL ack and canonical flush, restart, observe the same key/value reachable through canonical.
- Failure-path test: simulated flush failure does not report canonical success.

Depends on: sideshowdb-7pp (composite primitive)

title: Expose WriteThroughRefStore via CLI --refstore selector + acceptance
description:
Wire WriteThroughRefStore (and the composite cache form) into the CLI's --refstore selector + SIDESHOWDB_REFSTORE precedence chain so users can opt into write-through composite behavior from the command line.

Scope:
- Extend src/cli/refstore_selector.zig with a write-through composite selector accepting cache backend(s) + canonical backend (e.g. --refstore write-through:cache=memory,canonical=ziggit).
- Config TOML support in [storage].
- Gherkin acceptance scenarios under acceptance/typescript/features/ covering: enable via flag, enable via env, enable via config, precedence resolution, unknown backend rejection, mixed-cache fan-out happy path.
- EARS statements for the user-facing flag surface.

Why:
- Issue sideshowdb-7pp shipped WriteThroughRefStore as a library construct. The user-facing CLI surface is a separable concern with its own EARS + acceptance burden.

Depends on: sideshowdb-7pp

title: WriteThroughRefStore: instrumentation + metrics hooks
description:
Add structured instrumentation hooks to WriteThroughRefStore so operators can observe queue depth, flush latency, error rates, and per-cache fan-out timing without bolting on ad-hoc logging.

Scope:
- Define a small Metrics interface (counter + histogram + gauge primitives) on RefStore-style vtable shape so the WriteThrough store can emit events without hard-coding a sink.
- Wire emission for: enqueued, flushed, flush_failed, replayed, cache_fanout_ok, cache_fanout_failed (per-cache).
- Provide a no-op sink and an in-memory test sink.
- Bridge to an actual sink (stderr structured log? OpenTelemetry?) is a separate follow-up — keep this issue to the abstraction + emission only.

Why:
- Composite WriteThroughRefStore is the natural place to benchmark cache backends (LevelDB vs RocksDB vs in-memory). Real benchmarking demands real instrumentation. Without this, perf claims are unfalsifiable.

Depends on: sideshowdb-7pp

title: LevelDB FFI binding: implement on-disk LevelDbRefStore
description:
Add C FFI binding for LevelDB so WriteBehindRefStore can use a real on-disk cache backend.

Scope:
- Vendor or system-link c-leveldb (LevelDB's stable C wrapper).
- build.zig: add LevelDB target + C imports gated to non-freestanding native targets.
- src/core/storage/leveldb_ref_store.zig: implement RefStore vtable backed by a LevelDB DB handle. Map keys 1:1 (bytes), encode (value, version) tuples on disk.
- Pass the shared parity harness (tests/ref_store_parity.zig).
- Crash recovery: open existing DB and reproduce all prior puts with their version IDs intact.

Why:
- WriteBehindRefStore lands the architecture (queue + flush + recovery semantics) using MemoryRefStore as a placeholder cache. Real disk durability for the cache requires an actual disk-backed engine.
- LevelDB is the lighter end of the spectrum (single-process, broad ecosystem) compared to RocksDB.

Out of scope:
- WriteBehind queue logic itself (lives in WriteBehindRefStore).
- Multi-process safety beyond what LevelDB itself guarantees.

EARS:
- Where the LevelDB feature is enabled, the LevelDbRefStore shall persist puts, deletes, and version metadata to a LevelDB database directory provided at construction time.
- When a LevelDbRefStore opens an existing database, it shall reproduce the put/delete/version-id history that was committed in prior process lifetimes.
- If the LevelDB database is missing or corrupt, then the LevelDbRefStore shall return an open-time error and shall not silently truncate state.

Depends on: sideshowdb-7pp (WriteBehindRefStore composite slot)

title: LevelDB write-behind cache backend aligned with RocksDB storage layer
description:
Add LevelDB support as a write-behind local cache backend that occupies the same storage-architecture layer as RocksDB (disposable materialization/index layer, never canonical truth).

Why:
- Some environments benefit from a lightweight embedded KV option with broad ecosystem support and simpler deployment than RocksDB.
- The storage architecture already treats local engines (e.g., RocksDB, IndexedDB) as disposable indexes/materializations over Git-backed canonical state.
- A LevelDB backend gives a middle-ground durability/perf profile for native deployments and expands backend portability.

Scope:
- Introduce a LevelDB-backed cache/index component in the local materialization layer.
- Position it as write-behind relative to canonical Git/ref operations: staged writes are persisted locally first, then flushed/committed to canonical ref storage under defined ordering and failure semantics.
- Define backend selection and configuration surface alongside existing refstore/storage selection patterns.
- Specify crash-recovery and replay semantics so canonical truth remains reconstructible from Git even if local cache is lost/corrupt.
- Add tests for ordering, recovery, and no-loss-of-canonical invariants.
- Document when to choose LevelDB vs RocksDB.

Out of scope:
- Replacing Git as source of truth.
- Remote sync conflict policy changes.

EARS:
- Where LevelDB backend is enabled, the system shall treat LevelDB as a local write-behind materialization/cache layer and not as canonical truth.
- When a write is accepted into the LevelDB write-behind queue, the system shall flush the write to canonical Git/ref storage in deterministic order.
- If the LevelDB cache is deleted or corrupted, then the system shall rebuild local state from canonical Git/ref history without data loss of canonical records.
- If a write-behind flush fails, then the system shall surface a recoverable error state and shall not claim canonical commit success until Git/ref persistence succeeds.
- The system shall provide configuration and documentation that distinguish LevelDB and RocksDB backend trade-offs for native deployments.

Acceptance:
- Integration test: ordered writes flushed from LevelDB queue are reflected in canonical Git/ref history in the same order.
- Recovery test: deleting LevelDB cache and rehydrating reproduces state from canonical history.
- Failure-path test: simulated flush failure does not report canonical success and exposes recoverable status.
- Docs/spec update: storage architecture docs include LevelDB role and LevelDB-vs-RocksDB guidance.
notes: Scope split (2026-04-29): the original 7pp text bundled a 'composite cache slot' primitive with a 'WAL-batched async-flush' throughput optimization under the single phrase 'write-behind'. Per docs/development/decisions/2026-04-29-caching-model.md, this PR delivers ONLY the composite primitive as WriteThroughRefStore. The WAL-batched flush variant — including the original two queue/flush EARS — moved to sideshowdb-0r1. Reconciliation note: keep this issue scoped to WriteThroughRefStore + LevelDB-binding readiness; sideshowdb-frg owns the LevelDB FFI; sideshowdb-0r1 owns the WAL-batched async layer.
close_reason: Composite primitive delivered as WriteThroughRefStore in #21 + cleanup #22. Original write-behind queue/flush EARS migrated to sideshowdb-0r1; LevelDB FFI is sideshowdb-frg; sibling primitives p9h/nbv tracked separately. Scope-split notes preserved on this issue.

title: RocksDB-backed RefStore for native server scenarios
description:
Add a RocksDB-backed RefStore implementation for embedded/server deployments where Git on-disk format is undesirable but durability and high write throughput matter.

Why:
- Prior design discussions identified RocksDB as a target backend for native sideshowdb running as an embedded library or service.
- ZiggitRefStore (Git on disk) is good for portability and inspection, but not optimal for high write throughput or large key spaces.
- Pluggable RefStore backends are already part of the architecture; adding RocksDB exercises that boundary and unlocks the embedded-server use case.

Scope:
- Wire a Zig-friendly RocksDB build (cgo/zig-cc shim or existing Zig RocksDB binding) gated behind a build option (-Drefstore=rocksdb or similar) so default builds remain dependency-free.
- Implement RocksDBRefStore with the full RefStore vtable; map keys/versions onto column families or composite keys.
- CLI selector: '--refstore rocksdb' joins ziggit and subprocess, with config + env precedence already defined in storage spec.
- Tests: integration-level put/get/list/history/delete; reload across processes; benchmark suite (separate issue if needed).
- Docs: README + storage spec entry covering when to pick RocksDB vs ziggit.

Out of scope: replication (covered by remote sync issue).

Depends on: nothing hard-blocking, but should land after Option A so we ship MVP without a heavy dependency first.
notes: REST pivot: GitHubApiRefStore is now the primary remote-backed RefStore. Reposition this RocksDB backend as a native high-throughput materialization store, not primary remote storage.

title: WASM RefStore: ziggit-on-virtual-FS (Option B follow-up)
description:
Make ZiggitRefStore runnable inside wasm32-freestanding by abstracting its Platform.fs over a virtual in-memory filesystem.

Why:
- MemoryRefStore (Option A) gives WASM a working RefStore but loses the git-shaped on-disk layout that ziggit produces natively.
- A virtual-FS port lets the same in-process Git RefStore run in browser, preserving objects/refs structure for parity with native and enabling later browser-side push to a real remote.

Scope:
- Refactor ziggit_ref_store.zig so platform_instance is an injectable parameter rather than a file-scope const tied to std.Io.Dir.
- Implement a wasm-friendly platform impl backed by a std.StringHashMap-style virtual FS (paths -> bytes) using std.heap.wasm_allocator.
- Add wasm32-freestanding compile gate that selects the virtual FS platform.
- Tests: native runs ziggit on tmp dir (existing); freestanding test target runs ziggit on virtual FS and exercises put/get/history.

Out of scope: persistence to IndexedDB (covered by separate sync issue).

Depends on Option A landing first.
close_reason: Removed per docs/design/adrs/2026-04-29-deprecate-ziggit.md

title: Rename build steps to colon-separated lowercase (site:dev, check:core-docs)
description:
Build step strings currently mix lowercase singletons (run, test, wasm, site) with camelCase compounds (siteAssets, siteOnly, siteDev, sitePreview, siteReferenceDocs, siteInstall, checkCoreDocs). Zig stdlib precedent and user preference is single-word lowercase. Zig build has no nested subcommand parser, but colon-separated names cleanly mimic the pattern.

Proposed rename:
- siteAssets         -> site:assets
- siteReferenceDocs  -> site:reference
- siteOnly           -> site:build
- siteDev            -> site:dev
- sitePreview        -> site:preview
- siteInstall        -> site:install
- checkCoreDocs      -> check:core-docs

The existing single-word steps (run, test, wasm, site, siteInstall) stay. 'site' remains the default aggregator.

EARS:
- Where the build graph exposes a compound step, the step shall use colon-separated lowercase names (e.g. site:dev) instead of camelCase.
- When the rename lands, the assemble-pages-artifact.sh / verify-pages-artifact.sh / CI workflows shall be updated to invoke the new names.

Acceptance:
- All compound build steps use colon-separated lowercase.
- CI (.github/workflows/ci.yml + pages.yml) invokes new names.
- Local docs (CLAUDE.md, README, etc.) reference new names where mentioned.
- 'zig build' help output shows the new naming.
acceptance_criteria: All compound build steps use colon-separated lowercase. CI and docs updated. zig build help reflects new names.

WriteThroughRefStore: benchmark harness across cache backends
description:
Add a benchmark harness that drives WriteThroughRefStore through a configurable workload (put-heavy, get-heavy, mixed, large-value, many-keys) against each available cache backend (memory, LevelDB, RocksDB, Zeno) and reports per-op latency distributions, queue-depth percentiles, and flush throughput.

Scope:
- Standalone zig build target (e.g. zig build bench:write-through) that runs the same workload against each registered backend and prints comparable numbers.
- Reuses the metrics hook (sibling issue) for queue/flush stats.
- Documents methodology: warm-up rounds, run count, GC/page-cache caveats.

Out of scope:
- Continuous benchmarking in CI (do that once results stabilise).
- Cross-machine comparison harness.

Depends on: sideshowdb-7pp + LevelDB binding + instrumentation issues

title: WASM RefStore: WASI preview1 target with browser FS shim (Option C follow-up)
description:
Investigate switching the browser WASM build to wasm32-wasi (preview1) so ZiggitRefStore can run unchanged against a host-provided FS shim.

Why:
- Avoids forking ziggit's Platform.fs.
- Lets us pick existing browser WASI shims (e.g. wasi-virtual-fs) and reuse the native ziggit code path.

Scope (spike):
- Prototype build target wasm32-wasi alongside wasm32-freestanding.
- Pick a browser-side WASI runtime/shim, hook it up in loadSideshowdbClient.
- Compare bundle size and wiring cost vs Option B.
- Decide go/no-go; if go, file follow-up implementation issue.

Depends on Option A; Option B may obviate this.
notes:
2026-04-29: wasm32-wasi build target landed (commit b47b0a0).
- New build step: `zig build wasm-wasi` -> zig-out/wasm/sideshowdb-wasi.wasm
- Freestanding step `zig build wasm` unchanged -> sideshowdb.wasm
- src/wasm/root.zig now selects allocator per target (wasm_allocator on freestanding, page_allocator on wasi); same export surface.
- Bundle size (Debug): freestanding 1.41 MB, wasi 2.02 MB. WASI build imports the full wasi_snapshot_preview1 surface (fd_*, path_*, clock_*, etc.) — browser shim still TODO.
- Native `zig build test` green.

Remaining for go/no-go (still open scope of this issue):
- Pick a browser-side WASI shim (wasi-virtual-fs or similar) and wire into bindings/typescript loadSideshowdbClient.
- Actually attempt to run ZiggitRefStore unmodified against the shim FS.
- Compare runtime size + wiring cost vs Option B (sideshowdb-dgz).
